3: <header>5.2.1 Solving egg yolk powder production allocation </header>

In this case, the egg yolk supplier is a company in the food industry. It produces egg yolk powder and other food powders, including egg white powders. Regarding allocation issues at this life cycle phase, there is an issue with the egg white and egg shells, but also between other dried food products. As with the spent laying hens, these co-products could be allocated
either by the co-products' economic or mass value compared to the egg yolk product, which is the product of interest.

However, an estimation is that 11 percent of the input energy and materials should be allocated to the egg yolk powder (Fägerlind, 2013). Such a number is estimated taking into account the dry solid content of the egg yolk, number of production phases it passes, if the egg shell is removed at the supplier, the BOD7 and phosphorus content in the final product in comparison with other products (Fägerlind, 2013). According to the staff at this production site, such egg yolk powder burden is more representative than mass allocation, which could be done with the mass data presented in
Table 12. A scenario is presented in section 7.5, which tests allocation by mass. Economic allocation has not been considered in this case due to lack of trustworthy data.
Table 12. Egg products from egg yolk powder supplier.
Product|Share|Reference The egg white and mixed egg products|58%|(Fägerlind, 2013) Egg yolk|25%|(Fägerlind, 2013) Egg shells|11%|(Fägerlind, 2013) Egg residues|6%|(Fägerlind, 2013)

5: <header>5.3 Egg phospholipids production </header>

The egg phospholipids production at Fresenius-Kabi in Kungsängen requires around 500 000 American, German and Swedish chickens, which lay approximately 155 million eggs that give 180 tonnes egg phospholipids per year. It corresponds to approximately ten per cent of the total Swedish egg production.

The production of egg phospholipids are conducted batch-wise. Fresenius-Kabi completed 512 batches in the year of 2012. One batch gives 330 kilograms of egg phospholipids. The presented tables in this section correspond to energy and material need for one batch, which give 330 kilogram egg phospholipids, which also is the functional unit. All inputs for one batch are presented in Table 13.

2: <header>5.3.1 Solving egg yolk powder production allocation </header>

Since the phospholipids content in the egg yolk only sums up to 20 percent, there is a large flow of egg residue. The remaining 80 percent is termed defatted egg yolk powder (DEYP) and ends up in biogas production and in feed production, which create allocation issues.

In order to account for this allocation situation, the system boundaries could be expanded to include this biogas and feed production facilities to calculate a biogas and feed yield from the egg yolk residue. These yields are then replacing other production. Applied in this project, it credits the egg phospholipids production with the environmental load that other systems avoid by using its co-products. This form of system expansion is recommended according to the ISO 14040 standard. It is justified by the need of less energy and material in other systems since it replaces raw materials (Baumann & Tillman, 2004). Figure 2 shows how the system has expanded due to allocation issues.
Furthermore, there are heat residues and waste water sludge, which also becomes biogas that forms other allocation issues. These can be solved similarly as the egg yolk residues.

The recycled solvents create a more complicated allocation issue. Baumann and Tillman (2004) discuss different possibilities. A problem with recycled materials and LCA methodology is which recycled product that should be responsible for the environmental effects that occurs in the very first raw material acquisition and the last waste management. One approach is to consider that raw material acquisition always generates waste, and therefore the first product should hold the final waste management load. It could also be

argued that the waste management process creates a new need of raw materials, therefore the last product should hold the effects generated at the raw material acquisition. The first and the last product could also hold equal shares of both processes. Another option is to assign the impacts from waste management and raw material acquisition equally to all products that use the recycled material in their processes. This option is termed closed loop approximation and is suitable for materials that do not lose too much quality during the recycling.
The allocation issues that are given due to the byproducts biogas, heat and feed are solved by system expansion. The calculations behind the numbers presented in
Table 15 are presented in section 12.1.3.

5: <header>DEYP </header>

It is considered that the biogas is avoiding other biogas production. The defatted egg yolk powder (DEYP) is rich in fat and protein and the share that goes to biogas production gives a high biogas yield (Aronsson, 2012). The 388.25 m³ biogas yield presented in
Table 15 has been calculated with help from the wastewater treatment plant.

The DEYP feed share replaces another fat and protein source in another product. More than such information about its upstream production has not been able to obtain due to commercial confidentiality. This report uses the assumption that it replaces soybean cultivation and processing.
6: <header>Heat </header>

The heat is used in the local district heating system to heat nearby houses and buildings in Kungsängen. These houses and buildings would have obtained their heat from a wood pellets fueled heat plant, which it now avoids (Bengtsson, 2013).
7: <header>Egg phospholipids residue </header>

Approximately five per cent egg phospholipids, which corresponds to 17.5 kilograms, remain stuck in the production and do not enter the dispensing stage where the final packaging take place. This mass is returned to next batch. This fraction has been allocated by mass, which result in a 95 percent burden on the final product following Equation 3:
Equation 3. Egg phospholipids residue burden calculation EP stands for Egg Phospholipids.
17.5 kg EP residue =5% 330 kg EP final product + 17.5 kg EPresidue
8: <header>Recycled solvents </header>

The solvent recycling avoids solvent production. 21 011 kilograms ethanol are used per batch. 85.88 percent of this ethanol is recirculated to the redistillation process and reused in coming batches. 0.3 percent is combusted in the steam boilers. The company's ethanol balance lacks 13.82 percent ethanol per batch to be complete. Such fraction is considered to be emitted to the atmosphere.

The acetone faces a similar situation. 8 789 kilograms acetone are needed per batch. 86.99 percent of this acetone is redistillated and reused in coming batches. 13.01 percent is combusted in steam boilers which together with fuel oil, imported solvents cover the energy consumption per batch.

This allocation issue has been solved by only burdening one batch of egg phospholipids production with 14.12 and 13.01 percent of the total ethanol and acetone input. The energy input to the redistillation has also been accounted for.

There is a very small quality loss in the ethanol that is redistilled. Without physical ethanol losses (emissions to the atmosphere), the egg phospholipids production could be self- sufficient in ethanol (Lundström, 2013). 21 011 kilograms ethanol would last for approximately seven batches, and these batches do equally burden the energy input to the redistillation process and the impacts generated at the production of the ethanol. The performed allocation solution is also termed approximation with closed loop recycling.

0: Figure 4. Life cycle impact assessment normalized results.
1: <header>6.1 General emission sources and substances </header>

Egg production and egg phospholipids production are the two processes that stand out. The environmental impact that occurs at the egg yolk powder production is low in comparison with the other two processes. The impact categories ozone depletion, urban land occupation, water and metal depletion show very low global impact in relation to the total global emissions of this measure unit. These impact categories are therefore not further analyzed.

The ecotoxicity impact categories showed similar results, which originate from pesticide usage in the hen feed and ethanol feedstocks cultivation. In addition, since the ecotoxicity impact categories are measured with the same unit, only human toxicity entered to further analysis. In addition, the impact categories particulate matter formation, land occupation and natural land transformation are considered less relevant to Fresenius-Kabi.

Seven impact categories remain, which also reflect a wide set of actual environmental problems, where the egg production shows a greater impact in impact categories such as climate change, terrestrial acidification and marine eutrophication. The egg phospholipids production is responsible for most emissions in the impact categories human toxicity, photochemical oxidant formation and fossil depletion. The two processes equally burden the impact category freshwater eutrophication.

Relative the total global emissions, most impacts are generated in the ecotoxicity and eutrophication impact categories. This mirrors the production activities. The egg production and egg phospholipids production need agricultural land to cultivate raw materials to produce

hen feed and ethanol. In this raw material production, fertilizers, pesticides, lubricants and diesel usage generate these environmental impacts. Affects from usage of these raw materials are also detected in a study by Ometto, et al. (2009).

3: Figure 5. kg carbon dioxide equivalent emissions by process and substance.

As seen in Figure 5, the two greatest emission substances of carbon dioxide equivalents are carbon dioxide and dinitrogen monoxide. Two major emission sources of carbon dioxide are the combusted fuel oil in the steam boilers at the egg phospholipids production but also at the phase's raw material production. In addition, the vegetable fatty acids production is a greater emission source of carbon dioxide. Most dinitrogen monoxide emissions are generated at the hen feed production phase. It originates from the fertilizers production and usage within the hen feed production, especially from the dominating hen feed ingredients, soybean and wheat. Another emission source of dinitrogen oxides is the manure generated at the egg production. Note that the bar Free range egg production in Figure 5 only accounts for the free range egg production, which only accounts for 70% of the used eggs per functional unit. In addition, other activities in the cultivation and harvesting such as traction generate carbon dioxide emissions. The remaining carbon dioxide in the not specified process bar originates from raw material extraction and road transports, however, none of such process has larger impact than the other presented processes.
4: Figure 5. kg carbon dioxide equivalent emissions by process and substance.

As seen in Figure 5, the two greatest emission substances of carbon dioxide equivalents are carbon dioxide and dinitrogen monoxide. Two major emission sources of carbon dioxide are the combusted fuel oil in the steam boilers at the egg phospholipids production but also at the phase's raw material production. In addition, the vegetable fatty acids production is a greater emission source of carbon dioxide. Most dinitrogen monoxide emissions are generated at the hen feed production phase. It originates from the fertilizers production and usage within the hen feed production, especially from the dominating hen feed ingredients, soybean and wheat. Another emission source of dinitrogen oxides is the manure generated at the egg production. Note that the bar Free range egg production in Figure 5 only accounts for the free range egg production, which only accounts for 70% of the used eggs per functional unit. In addition, other activities in the cultivation and harvesting such as traction generate carbon dioxide emissions. The remaining carbon dioxide in the not specified process bar originates from raw material extraction and road transports, however, none of such process has larger impact than the other presented processes.

0: Figure 6. kg 1,4-dichlorobenzene equivalent emissions by process and substance.

Human toxicity is the impact category with the highest share to the global pollution among all impact categories in this study. The impact assessment method ReCiPe uses the chemical 1,4- dichlorobenzene as a reference substance to measure this impact category. The greatest emission substances sources in this impact category are manganese, phosphorus and arsenic. These emissions originate from fertilizer and pesticide usage in ethanol and vegetable fatty acids feedstocks cultivation. The manganese and phosphorus emissions to soil and air originate from the fertilizers, while arsenic leak from pesticide usage. The Swedish high voltage electricity generation and nitrogen gas production also generate impact in this category. The bar Not specified processes include processes like fossil fuels managing in transports and tractor usage. Other not mentioned emission substances are heavy metals such as barium, cadmium and lead.
Figure 7. kg non methane volatile organic compounds equivalents emissions by process and substance.

The main emission substances in this impact category are NMVOC, nitrogen oxides and carbon monoxide. Most NMVOC emits from the egg phospholipids production, where leakage of ethanol generate these emissions. The ethanol production is another great emission source of NMVOC equivalent emissions, but the emission substance source is mostly carbon monoxide. Traction used at the hen feed production generates some emissions in this impact category due to its use of diesel. Other not presented processes are represented by fossil fuels handling in for example transports.
Figure 8. kg sulfur dioxide equivalent emissions by process and substance.

This impact category dominates by the emission substances ammonia emissions, but also nitrogen and sulfur oxides emissions. The high ammonia emissions derive from the hen manure generated in the egg production, but also from other processes which manage fertilizers, such as cultivation of feedstocks for production of hen feed and ethanol. The nitrogen and sulfur oxides originate from fossil fuel usage in for example tractor usage. In addition, transport generate emissions of nitrogen and sulfur oxides in this category, where the freighter transport of soybeans from Brazil to Norway is the most energy demanding transports, due to its distance but also to its high share in the hen feed.
Figure 9. kg phosphorus equivalent emissions by process and substance.

The dominating emission substance sources in the freshwater eutrophication impact category are phosphate and phosphorus. The major emission sources of phosphate are the hen manure management and the fertilizer usage in the hen feed and ethanol production. The main emission source of phosphorus is the waste water from egg yolk powder and egg phospholipids production. In addition, the nitrogen gas production also contributes to the emission in this impact category.
Figure 10. kg nitrogen equivalent emissions by process and substance.

Also the marine eutrophication impact category finds its emission sources in the hen feed fertilizers and hen manure, where nitrate and ammonia leak. Especially wheat production contributes with high nitrate emissions since it is the main ingredient in the hen feed. Nitrate is the dominating emission substance source. Nitrogen oxides emissions to air from diesel use in tractors when harvesting the feed ingredients give a lower impact.

The raw materials used in egg phospholipids production and the production's waste water are its emission sources in this impact category. However, the egg phospholipids production life cycle phase is no net producer of nitrogen equivalent emissions due to the avoided fertilizer usage in the avoided soybeans production.
Figure 11. kg crude oil equivalents emissions by process and substance.

The use of fossil fuels is concentrated to the fuel oil usage in steam boilers at the egg phospholipids production and egg yolk powder production. Acetone and ethanol production requires both natural gas and crude oil. It is manufactured in Germany, therefore typical Swedish energy carriers are not used. Diesel usage within the feed transportation and traction in hen feed production also adds on to crude oil acquisition. Some natural gas is also used in the cultivation of soybeans and sugar cane in Brazil. An interesting point is that acetone is more fossil fuel demanding than ethanol even though more ethanol is needed per functional unit.

5: <header>7. Sensitivity analysis </header>
Five scenarios were conducted in order to verify the validity of the results and to explore how other production methods change the environmental impact of the egg phospholipids.

In the first scenario, the product was based on only cage hen eggs. Since great impact was encountered in the hen feed ingredients production, and cage hens consume less feed than free-range hens, this was found to be interesting. The hen feed ingredient production methods are not analyzed in this thesis, but could certainly also be considered.

Ethanol production from sugar cane molasses shows high impacts in several impact categories, therefore it would be interesting to analyze how other ethanol types changes the environmental load of the egg phospholipids.

38 percent of the defatted egg yolk powder (DEYP) is in the base case scenario treated in a biogas facility and the remaining 62 percent was further processed by a supplier to replace protein and fat sources. In order to see which treatment method credits the egg phospholipids the most, the treatment method for the DEYP was analyzed. The investigated treatment methods were biogas digestion and protein and fat source production in the third scenario.

In the fourth scenario, the validity of the chosen base case hen manure treatment method was tested. In the base case scenario, the chosen method was fertilizer production. However, the manure was also an excellent source in biogas production, and the differences between these two treatment methods was tested.

Finally, since the inventory analysis gave two possibilities on how to allocate impacts between egg yolk powder and other products generated in that life cycle phase, the last scenario analyzes the results when changing the allocation factor for the egg yolk powder.

38 percent of the defatted egg yolk powder (DEYP) is in the base case scenario treated in a biogas facility and the remaining 62 percent was further processed by a supplier to replace protein and fat sources. In order to see which treatment method credits the egg phospholipids the most, the treatment method for the DEYP was analyzed. The investigated treatment methods were biogas digestion and protein and fat source production in the third scenario.

However, the manure was also an excellent source in biogas production, and the differences between these two treatment methods was tested.

7: <header>7.1 Cage hen egg scenario </header>

Cage hens are in the base case scenario assumed to eat 0.2 kilograms less feed per kilograms of produced eggs, hence, this scenario put less stress on the hen feed cultivation processes. As with the base case scenario, this scenario presupposes 913 cage hens that produce 20 kilograms of eggs per life. The cage hens require two kilograms of feed per kilograms of eggs, which result in a total requirement of 36 520 kilograms hen feed. In the base case scenario, the hen feed input is 39 076 kilograms, which gives 2.14 kilograms of hen feed per kilograms of eggs. The hen feed input to this scenario is 6.5 percent lower.

In addition, lower mass amounts not only affect the cultivation, but also the transports needed to freight the feed ingredients. The transports are reduced with 6.5 percent, as with the total hen feed input. The characterized results from this cage hen egg scenario are presented in Table 18.

0: <title>Table 18. Characterized results from cage hen scenario. </title>

Impact category|Unit|Cage hen scenario|Total reduction|Reduction [%] Climate change|kg CO2 eq|39 693,15|-1 534,44|-5% Human toxicity|kg 1,4-DB eq|8 134,94|-108,11|-5% Photochemical oxidant formation|kg NMVOC|445,48|-6,42|-6% Terrestrial acidification|kg SO2 eq|551,65|-10,61|-2% Freshwater eutrophication|kg P eq|8,42|-0,11|-3% Marine eutrophication|kg N eq|333,97|-9,63|-3% Fossil depletion|kg oil eq|072,18|-211,16|-6%

Lowering the feed intake by 0.14 kilograms reduces the environmental impact from the hen feed production phase with two to six percent in the different impact categories. Less need of fertilizers give less dinitrogen oxide emissions, which decrease the carbon dioxide equivalents. The reduction in the acidification and eutrophication impact categories are lower due to most effects in those impact categories being generated by the hen manure, which is not assumed to differ due to egg production system.

2: <header>7.2 Ethanol scenario </header>

Molasses ethanol is derived from a sugar feedstock and a co-product recovery, which is one of several feedstocks for ethanol production (Wilkie, et al., 2000). Other common feedstocks are starch and cellulose. Residue materials as feedstocks, which also include sugar cane molasses, show interesting features since it creates allocation situations where all impacts are not carried by a single product. Another advantage is that residue agricultural products are not subject to food production. Other examples of such residue materials than molasses are pulp from the paper industry and agricultural crop residues such as corn stover and straw from wheat production, which remain after harvest (Nelson, 2007).

The molasses are a co-product from sugar crystallization, which is a sub process in the sugar cane production. There are a few other co-products from sugar production, and SO all inputs to sugar production should be allocated between these different co-products. The ethanol from the molasses should not by itself carry all environmental impacts from the cultivation.

Ethanol production from cellulosic feedstocks has shown promising results, due to lower fertilizer and pesticide need, energy ratio, availability in several climate zones, its ethanol yields and certain features of the plants (Sánchez & Cardona, 2008). A study by Kusiima and Powers (2009) which compared ethanol production from different feedstocks such as corn (starch), corn stover, switchgrass, and forest residue (cellulose) concluded that the lower fertilizer need by cellulosic feedstocks resulted in lower upstream emissions (Kusiima & Powers, 2010). Another study by Somma et al. (2009) concluded that cellulosic feedstocks have better energy conversion ratios compared with corn (starch) and sugar cane (sugar), which reduce carbon dioxide emissions, and result in less damaging land and water impacts. (Somma, et al., 2009)
In this scenario, the sugar cane molasses ethanol is substituted by two other ethanol types. The two chosen ethanol types are produced from the feedstocks switchgrass and wood. Since also

switchgrass originates from cultivation, some similar environmental issues are expected compared to the base case scenario. Switchgrass is a fast growing crop, which grows to around two meters high. It requires little water or fertilizer to grow (Montenegro, 2005). Switchgrass also has a good ability to extract nitrogen from unfertilized soils. Parrish and Fike (2005) reported in a study that very little fertilizer is applied in its cultivation, which resulted in a net removal of nitrogen (Parrish & Fike, 2005).

Changing feedstocks type to cellulose based ethanol may also change the origin of the ethanol. However, transports are not changed in this scenario since its aim is to investigate the impacts from ethanol production. In addition, this thesis does not aim to find possible ethanol suppliers. The characterized results from this ethanol scenario are presented in Table 19.
Table 19. Characterized results from ethanol scenario.

Impact category|Unit|Wood ethanol|Total reduction|Variation [%]|Grass ethanol|Total reduction|Variation [%] Climate change|kg CO2 eq|41 399|172|0,42%|42 009|781|1,90% Human toxicity|kg 1,4-DB eq|6 702|-1 1542|-19%|455|-3 787|-46% Photochemical oxidant formation|kg NMVOC|365|-87|-19%|362|-90|-20% Terrestrial acidification|kg SO2 eq|547|-15|-2,63%|630|68|12% Freshwater eutrophication|kg P eq|7,70|-0,83|-9,71%|8,10|-0,43|-4,99% Marine eutrophication|kg N eq|341|-2,17|-0,63%|347|3,64|1,06% Fossil depletion|kg oil eq|9 377|94|1,01%|9 239|-44|-0,47%

Table 19 shows low differences in the impact categories such as climate change, terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication and fossil depletion. However, the impact category human toxicity show clearly lower emissions from wood and grass based ethanol.

The reason for the lower emissions of dichloride benzene equivalents from the grass ethanol is the result of lower phosphorus emissions from its cultivation, as seen in Figure 12. Also the wood ethanol has the lowest amounts of all emission substances. However, as Figure 13 shows, the ammonia emissions are higher from the grass ethanol, which generate high impact in the Terrestrial Acidification impact category. Regarding phosphorus equivalent emissions, the sugar cane molasses ethanol shows highest impact, which can be seen in Figure 14.

switchgrass originates from cultivation, some similar environmental issues are expected compared to the base case scenario. Switchgrass is a fast growing crop, which grows to around two meters high. It requires little water or fertilizer to grow (Montenegro, 2005). Switchgrass also has a good ability to extract nitrogen from unfertilized soils. Parrish and Fike (2005) reported in a study that very little fertilizer is applied in its cultivation, which resulted in a net removal of nitrogen (Parrish & Fike, 2005).

Changing feedstocks type to cellulose based ethanol may also change the origin of the ethanol. However, transports are not changed in this scenario since its aim is to investigate the impacts from ethanol production. In addition, this thesis does not aim to find possible ethanol suppliers. The characterized results from this ethanol scenario are presented in Table 19.
Table 19. Characterized results from ethanol scenario.

Impact category|Unit|Wood ethanol|Total reduction|Variation [%]|Grass ethanol|Total reduction|Variation [%] Climate change|kg CO2 eq|41 399|172|0,42%|42 009|781|1,90% Human toxicity|kg 1,4-DB eq|6 702|-1 1542|-19%|455|-3 787|-46% Photochemical oxidant formation|kg NMVOC|365|-87|-19%|362|-90|-20% Terrestrial acidification|kg SO2 eq|547|-15|-2,63%|630|68|12% Freshwater eutrophication|kg P eq|7,70|-0,83|-9,71%|8,10|-0,43|-4,99% Marine eutrophication|kg N eq|341|-2,17|-0,63%|347|3,64|1,06% Fossil depletion|kg oil eq|9 377|94|1,01%|9 239|-44|-0,47%

Table 19 shows low differences in the impact categories such as climate change, terrestrial acidification, marine eutrophication and fossil depletion. However, the impact category human toxicity show clearly lower emissions from wood and grass based ethanol.

The reason for the lower emissions of dichloride benzene equivalents from the grass ethanol is the result of lower phosphorus emissions from its cultivation, as seen in Figure 12. Also the wood ethanol has the lowest amounts of all emission substances. However, as Figure 13 shows, the ammonia emissions are higher from the grass ethanol, which generate high impact in the Terrestrial Acidification impact category. Regarding phosphorus equivalent emissions, the sugar cane molasses ethanol shows highest impact, which can be seen in Figure 14.

5: <header>7.2 Ethanol scenario </header>

Molasses ethanol is derived from a sugar feedstock and a co-product recovery, which is one of several feedstocks for ethanol production (Wilkie, et al., 2000). Other common feedstocks are starch and cellulose. Residue materials as feedstocks, which also include sugar cane molasses, show interesting features since it creates allocation situations where all impacts are not carried by a single product. Another advantage is that residue agricultural products are not subject to food production. Other examples of such residue materials than molasses are pulp from the paper industry and agricultural crop residues such as corn stover and straw from wheat production, which remain after harvest (Nelson, 2007).

The molasses are a co-product from sugar crystallization, which is a sub process in the sugar cane production. There are a few other co-products from sugar production, and SO all inputs to sugar production should be allocated between these different co-products. The ethanol from the molasses should not by itself carry all environmental impacts from the cultivation.

Ethanol production from cellulosic feedstocks has shown promising results, due to lower fertilizer and pesticide need, energy ratio, availability in several climate zones, its ethanol yields and certain features of the plants (Sánchez & Cardona, 2008). A study by Kusiima and Powers (2009) which compared ethanol production from different feedstocks such as corn (starch), corn stover, switchgrass, and forest residue (cellulose) concluded that the lower fertilizer need by cellulosic feedstocks resulted in lower upstream emissions (Kusiima & Powers, 2010). Another study by Somma et al. (2009) concluded that cellulosic feedstocks have better energy conversion ratios compared with corn (starch) and sugar cane (sugar), which reduce carbon dioxide emissions, and result in less damaging land and water impacts. (Somma, et al., 2009)
In this scenario, the sugar cane molasses ethanol is substituted by two other ethanol types. The two chosen ethanol types are produced from the feedstocks switchgrass and wood. Since also

switchgrass originates from cultivation, some similar environmental issues are expected compared to the base case scenario. Switchgrass is a fast growing crop, which grows to around two meters high. It requires little water or fertilizer to grow (Montenegro, 2005). Switchgrass also has a good ability to extract nitrogen from unfertilized soils. Parrish and Fike (2005) reported in a study that very little fertilizer is applied in its cultivation, which resulted in a net removal of nitrogen (Parrish & Fike, 2005).

Changing feedstocks type to cellulose based ethanol may also change the origin of the ethanol. However, transports are not changed in this scenario since its aim is to investigate the impacts from ethanol production. In addition, this thesis does not aim to find possible ethanol suppliers. The characterized results from this ethanol scenario are presented in Table 19.
Table 19. Characterized results from ethanol scenario.

2: <header>7.5 Egg yolk allocation factor scenario </header>

As explained in chapter 5.2.1, the base case scenario uses an allocation factor of 11 percent to divide the environmental burdens between different products generated at the egg yolk powder production. This scenario tests the mass allocation factor, which is 25 percent, as shown in
Table 12.

Only the washing is subject to allocation, the energy use is measured per kilogram of egg yolk powder. Some fuel oil is used in the washing, thus the fuel oil usage increases in this scenario. Therefore, only usage levels of water, waste water, lye, fuel oil, air and water emissions are affected by the higher allocation factor. Transports are also changed since more lye and fuel oil must be transported. The inputs that represent this scenario are presented in section 12.2. The characterized results from this scenario are presented in Table 22.
Table 22. Characterized results from egg yolk allocation factor scenario.

Impact category|Unit|Base case scenario|25% allocation factor|Base case scenario share of total emissions|25% allocation factor scenario share of total emissions|Variation [%] Climate change|kg CO2 eq|2 215,49|2 853,58|5,4%|6,8%|27% Human toxicity|kg 1,4-DB eq|204,27|271,42|2,5%|3,3%|32% Photochemical oxidant formation|kg NMVOC|8,49|11,39|1,9%|2,5%|33% Terrestrial acidification|kg SO2 eq|4,60|5,86|0,8%|1,0%|27% Freshwater eutrophication|kg P eq|0,57|1,14|6,7%|12,6%|88% Marine eutrophication|kg N eq|5,69|10,34|1,7%|3,0%|79% Fossil depletion|kg oil eq|614,26|934,87|6,6%|9,7%|47%

As seen in Table 22, this scenario generates greater emissions, which is expected since the allocation factor is increased from 11 percent to 25 percent. However, since the magnitude of the emission from the egg yolk powder production already was low, the higher emission levels do not significantly affect the total emissions.

5: <header>8.1 Methodological choices and data uncertainty </header>

Data uncertainty is a good start for LCA discussions. In this case, there are some steps that can possibly affect the reliability of the results. The impact assessment method is one such step. Recipe Midpoint Hierarchist World is the used impact assessment methodology in this

thesis. Other possible impact assessment methods for this thesis are for instance Ecoinvent 99 and CML 2000. All three were developed by Pré, who considers ReCiPe an improvement on Ecoinvent 99, thus it was chosen for this project (Pré, 2012).

The Hierarchist version is the moderate perspective. It's technological and policy making assumptions are neither optimistic nor pessimistic. The Hierarchist method is also considered as the default choice (Pré, 2012). The two other perspectives are considered as robustness analysis methods (Bare & Gloria, 2006).

Furthermore, the data uncertainty can be affected by the chosen classification and characterization approach. Midpoint evaluation has been used in this thesis. The endpoint impact categories are due to its greater specificity, considered to be more understandable and therefore also more relevant for decision makers (Bare, et al., 2000). However, even though the midpoint results are more complex they give a more nuanced picture. Since the main target group of this thesis is the environmental department at Fresenius-Kabi, where environmental knowledge is high, the decision can be justified by the target group. In addition, the higher uncertainty in the endpoint analysis is another justification to such choice. To estimate the endpoint impact categories involves several different factors such as human health statistics and disability-adjusted life year (DALY) statistics of different diseases such as cancer. This makes DALY calculations dependent on subjective assumptions; it must accept how human health reacts to world average (Goedkoop, et al., 2009). This complicates endpoint estimation and gives it a higher uncertainty.

Data uncertainty is a greater problem in the background processes, where less detailed literature review has been performed compared to the foreground system for this thesis. However, it is considered that the data in the used databases are high quality and passed a quality check to reach general acceptance. The results can be seen as no absolute numbers, but an indication and a trend on the situation.

The used database for most hen feed products are from the Food LCA DK database, which is based on Danish production systems. This could be another source of uncertainty. In the SimaPro modeling, Danish electricity has been substituted with Swedish electricity.

Some non-mandatory LCA steps such as grouping and weighting are not performed in this thesis. As with endpoint evaluation, which gives more specific result but also less diverse result, weighting and grouping are also considered to give less diverse results. The target group has a high understanding of different environmental issues, and can interpret the results. The study is neither a comparison study, where weighting is commonly performed. In addition, the development of the weights is believed to increase the level of subjectivity in the study.

Regarding the performed allocation solutions, it is generally considered that system expansion should be used when dealing with allocation issues. In this thesis, four of nine encountered allocation issues have been solved by such method. The spent laying hens and egg phospholipids residues could be allocated similarly, however, no usage area for the meat has been found, and since other studies allocated by mass, this study did not either expand the
system boundaries. In addition, extensive and quality data are other reasons to why these issues have been solved by mass allocation.

The recycled solvents are allocated by approximation with closed loop recycling. It is considered that recycled materials that lose little quality should be allocated with such method. It is considered reasonable that recycled materials should equally burden the environmental impacts at raw material and waste handling.

The allocation issue that occurs in the egg yolk powder production is subject to greater discussion. Mass allocation is not appropriate in this case since the egg white has higher water content, and therefore also will require more energy to dry. Economic allocation could account for such higher energy need, but has not been considered optimal by the supplier. The allocation percentage that is used in this project is derived by experts in the egg yolk powder industry. In addition, a scenario has been performed that concluded that increasing the burden of the egg yolk powder production did not significantly affect the results.

The greatest transport in terms of ton kilometers (tkm) is the transport of soybeans from Brazil to Perstorp via Fredrikstad and Hällekis. This transport does not show any greater impact. Transports do not generate a greater impact in the life cycle of egg phospholipids. The great need of raw materials to produce the functional unit creates greater impact at their manufacturing, than to transport them despite the long distances. Regarding the assumptions that have been done for the transports, they can be said not to be overestimated, since no transport show any greater impact. The assumption about which kind of feed source that the egg yolk residues from the egg phospholipids production replace can be justified by the high usage of soybeans as protein and fat sources in animal feeds (Lovén Persson, 2009).

The chosen impact categories reflect the interest of Fresenius-Kabi. Eleven impact categories have been excluded from the analysis, either by its low global impact or due to the interest of the employer.
6: <header>8.1 Methodological choices and data uncertainty </header>

Data uncertainty is a good start for LCA discussions. In this case, there are some steps that can possibly affect the reliability of the results. The impact assessment method is one such step. Recipe Midpoint Hierarchist World is the used impact assessment methodology in this

thesis. Other possible impact assessment methods for this thesis are for instance Ecoinvent 99 and CML 2000. All three were developed by Pré, who considers ReCiPe an improvement on Ecoinvent 99, thus it was chosen for this project (Pré, 2012).

The Hierarchist version is the moderate perspective. It's technological and policy making assumptions are neither optimistic nor pessimistic. The Hierarchist method is also considered as the default choice (Pré, 2012). The two other perspectives are considered as robustness analysis methods (Bare & Gloria, 2006).

Furthermore, the data uncertainty can be affected by the chosen classification and characterization approach. Midpoint evaluation has been used in this thesis. The endpoint impact categories are due to its greater specificity, considered to be more understandable and therefore also more relevant for decision makers (Bare, et al., 2000). However, even though the midpoint results are more complex they give a more nuanced picture. Since the main target group of this thesis is the environmental department at Fresenius-Kabi, where environmental knowledge is high, the decision can be justified by the target group. In addition, the higher uncertainty in the endpoint analysis is another justification to such choice. To estimate the endpoint impact categories involves several different factors such as human health statistics and disability-adjusted life year (DALY) statistics of different diseases such as cancer. This makes DALY calculations dependent on subjective assumptions; it must accept how human health reacts to world average (Goedkoop, et al., 2009). This complicates endpoint estimation and gives it a higher uncertainty.

Data uncertainty is a greater problem in the background processes, where less detailed literature review has been performed compared to the foreground system for this thesis. However, it is considered that the data in the used databases are high quality and passed a quality check to reach general acceptance. The results can be seen as no absolute numbers, but an indication and a trend on the situation.

The used database for most hen feed products are from the Food LCA DK database, which is based on Danish production systems. This could be another source of uncertainty. In the SimaPro modeling, Danish electricity has been substituted with Swedish electricity.

Some non-mandatory LCA steps such as grouping and weighting are not performed in this thesis. As with endpoint evaluation, which gives more specific result but also less diverse result, weighting and grouping are also considered to give less diverse results. 11: <header>8.2 Life cycle impact assessment results </header>

The results presented in this theses show similar result as earlier studies and expectations. The worst environmental impacts are found in ecotoxicity and eutrophication impact categories. Earlier LCA-studies on egg production have encountered great emissions sources in the hen feed production and hen manure management due to nitrate and ammonia emissions. These emission sources are also detected in this thesis. The worst detected environmental concern at Fresenius-Kabi is the NMVOC emissions. Compared to other life cycle phases, the egg phospholipids production clearly stands out as one of the greater emission sources of NMVOC in the particulate matter formation impact category.

Perhaps the high global impact in the ecotoxicity impact categories is surprising. However, none of the used references on egg production has analyzed it in deeper detail. Regarding the major ecotoxicity impact emission sources, they are encountered in Brazil, where environmental regulation on pesticide usage has not yet reached the equivalent level in developed countries (Rosén Nilsson & Tengnäs, 2002).

In addition, the low effect on the water footprint is perhaps surprising. A study done by Hoekstra and Mekonnen (2010) conclude that the water footprint of chicken egg is calculated to be around 3 300 cubic meters per ton eggs. Another life cycle phase that requires vast water amounts is the bioethanol production.

Hen feed production requires higher raw materials quantities than ethanol production; hence, it shows greater impacts in some impact categories. The sugar cane cultivation does not require as much land as for example wheat (Sánchez & Cardona, 2008).

Human ecotoxicity is the impact category with the highest contribution to global pollution. Several raw materials that are used within the life cycle of egg phospholipids originate from cultivation, where pesticides and fertilizers leak manganese, phosphorus and heavy metals. Manganese is toxic to humans and can lead to diseases of motion, reproduction and respiratory disturbances (Francis & Forsyth, 1995). Regarding phosphorus it refers to white phosphorus, which is very toxic to humans. It can for example give respiratory problems and affect organs such as the liver and kidneys (ATSDR, 1997). The eutrophication impact categories also show high impact, where phosphate and nitrate emissions from the hen manure and fertilizers is a great emission source.

The production and management of hen feed, the hen manure, ethanol and the final production of egg phospholipids are considered to be the major process sources of environmental pollution in the life cycle of egg phospholipids. The hen feed production and the hen manure management are processes that have a greater impact in impact categories such as climate change, acidification and eutrophication. Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from manure and phosphate and nitrate emissions from fertilizers are the major emissions substance sources from hen feed production and hen manure management. Ethanol production generates impact in the impact categories, human toxicity, eutrophication and fossil depletion, due to emissions from fertilizers and pesticides. The production also requires fossil fuels, which add on to its depletion. The egg phospholipids production is a greater emission source of carbon dioxide emissions due to incineration of fuel oil in steam boilers. NMVOC, phosphate and phosphorus are other major emissions substance sources from the egg phospholipids production, which give the phase presence in the impact categories climate change, photochemical oxidant formation and freshwater eutrophication. Below are the emission sources and substances per impact category explained in detail.

Transports show low impact. Even though most transport use diesel, no major effects from its fossil fuel usage are shown. Efficient and extensive packaging gives low impact per weight unit of material. Compared to ethanol, less acetone is used per functional unit. However, in the fossil depletion impact category, the acetone show higher usage levels of fossil fuels. Acetone production is more fossil fuel demanding per mass unit than ethanol production.

The results show impact in relation to its input mass; they do not relate environmental impact of equal amounts to each other. Since more kilograms of ethanol are used than acetone, it is in most cases a greater impact source. To lower the environmental burden of the product,
environmental impact per mass unit of raw material could be more useful in order to choose raw materials with low environmental impact.

However, in the fossil depletion impact category, the acetone show higher usage levels of fossil fuels. Acetone production is more fossil fuel demanding per mass unit than ethanol production.

The results show impact in relation to its input mass; they do not relate environmental impact of equal amounts to each other. Since more kilograms of ethanol are used than acetone, it is in most cases a greater impact source. To lower the environmental burden of the product,
environmental impact per mass unit of raw material could be more useful in order to choose raw materials with low environmental impact.
13: <header>8.2 Life cycle impact assessment results </header>

The results presented in this theses show similar result as earlier studies and expectations. The worst environmental impacts are found in ecotoxicity and eutrophication impact categories. Earlier LCA-studies on egg production have encountered great emissions sources in the hen feed production and hen manure management due to nitrate and ammonia emissions. These emission sources are also detected in this thesis. The worst detected environmental concern at Fresenius-Kabi is the NMVOC emissions. Compared to other life cycle phases, the egg phospholipids production clearly stands out as one of the greater emission sources of NMVOC in the particulate matter formation impact category.

Perhaps the high global impact in the ecotoxicity impact categories is surprising. However, none of the used references on egg production has analyzed it in deeper detail. Regarding the major ecotoxicity impact emission sources, they are encountered in Brazil, where environmental regulation on pesticide usage has not yet reached the equivalent level in developed countries (Rosén Nilsson & Tengnäs, 2002).

In addition, the low effect on the water footprint is perhaps surprising. A study done by Hoekstra and Mekonnen (2010) conclude that the water footprint of chicken egg is calculated to be around 3 300 cubic meters per ton eggs. Another life cycle phase that requires vast water amounts is the bioethanol production.

Hen feed production requires higher raw materials quantities than ethanol production; hence, it shows greater impacts in some impact categories. The sugar cane cultivation does not require as much land as for example wheat (Sánchez & Cardona, 2008).

Human ecotoxicity is the impact category with the highest contribution to global pollution. Several raw materials that are used within the life cycle of egg phospholipids originate from cultivation, where pesticides and fertilizers leak manganese, phosphorus and heavy metals. Manganese is toxic to humans and can lead to diseases of motion, reproduction and respiratory disturbances (Francis & Forsyth, 1995). Regarding phosphorus it refers to white phosphorus, which is very toxic to humans. It can for example give respiratory problems and affect organs such as the liver and kidneys (ATSDR, 1997). The eutrophication impact categories also show high impact, where phosphate and nitrate emissions from the hen manure and fertilizers is a great emission source.

The production and management of hen feed, the hen manure, ethanol and the final production of egg phospholipids are considered to be the major process sources of environmental pollution in the life cycle of egg phospholipids. The hen feed production and the hen manure management are processes that have a greater impact in impact categories such as climate change, acidification and eutrophication. Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from manure and phosphate and nitrate emissions from fertilizers are the major emissions substance sources from hen feed production and hen manure management. Ethanol production generates impact in the impact categories, human toxicity, eutrophication and fossil depletion, due to emissions from fertilizers and pesticides. The production also requires fossil fuels, which add on to its depletion. The egg phospholipids production is a greater emission source of carbon dioxide emissions due to incineration of fuel oil in steam boilers. NMVOC, phosphate and phosphorus are other major emissions substance sources from the egg phospholipids production, which give the phase presence in the impact categories climate change, photochemical oxidant formation and freshwater eutrophication. Below are the emission sources and substances per impact category explained in detail.

Transports show low impact. Even though most transport use diesel, no major effects from its fossil fuel usage are shown. Efficient and extensive packaging gives low impact per weight unit of material. Compared to ethanol, less acetone is used per functional unit. However, in the fossil depletion impact category, the acetone show higher usage levels of fossil fuels. Acetone production is more fossil fuel demanding per mass unit than ethanol production.

The results show impact in relation to its input mass; they do not relate environmental impact of equal amounts to each other. Since more kilograms of ethanol are used than acetone, it is in most cases a greater impact source. To lower the environmental burden of the product,
environmental impact per mass unit of raw material could be more useful in order to choose raw materials with low environmental impact.

16: In order to decrease the environmental impact from egg phospholipids production, five scenarios have been studied. The hen feed production occupies a high environmental impact of the egg phospholipids. By lowering the feed intake with 0.14 kilograms hen feed per kilograms of egg lower the characterized results by two to six percent in the different impact categories. However, lowering the feed intake per kilograms must not affect the nutrient needs of the hens and their health, but since the hen feed production shows high impact, it can certainly be studied in order to reach an optimal feeding intake. Lovén Persson (2009) discuss how increased number of feeding occations per day decrease the feed spill. Bigger feed distribution boxes also deminish the congestion, and the hens can intake the feed less stressfully. Requirements can also be demanded on the fertilizer usage in the feed production.

Free range hens have a higher ability to express their natural behavior, which may be an ethical reason to choose eggs from free range systems. They can stretch their wings, exercise their muscles and benefit from the outdoors in a way that cage hens never can.

Regarding the environmental impact from ethanol production, the cellulose feedstocks show a clear lower impact in the ecotoxicity and eutrophication impact categories. In this thesis, the capacity or price of ethanol production from cellulose feedstocks is not discussed and could be an obstacle.

The egg phospholipids production can receive more credits when its co-product egg yolk residue avoids soybean cultivation compared to biogas production. The forth scenario shows little differences and no clear best alternative for hen manure treatment method has been found.

The egg phospholipids production can receive more credits when its co-product egg yolk residue avoids soybean cultivation compared to biogas production. The forth scenario shows little differences and no clear best alternative for hen manure treatment method has been found.
18: Four sources have been detected as severe emission sources that cause the major environmental impact in the life cycle of the egg phospholipids. These are: hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production, hen manure management and the final egg phospholipids production. Fresenius-Kabi has only direct control over its own final egg phospholipids production. That phase is therefore the only one that the company can directly act upon.

The solvent emissions from the egg phospholipids production is the major environmental problem at the commodity plant in Kungsängen. It is also a greater emission source in the life cycle of egg phospholipids, thus actions that decrease these emissions will also have effects in a life cycle perspective. The carbon dioxide emission from the steam boilers is not as clear emission source as the solvents in a life cycle perspective.

The three other emission sources cannot directly be affected by Fresenius-Kabi. However, the company can demand that certain production conditions are fulfilled or ensured. When analyzing ethanol feedstocks the ethanol yield per square meter is an essential parameter,
which lowers energy, pesticide and fertilizer usage per liter ethanol. Also, the crop properties could be interesting; since some crops are less dependent on fertilizers and pesticides.

Regarding the egg production, requirements should be imposed on the egg yolk powder supplier to only purchase eggs from hen laying farms that work actively to decrease their environmental impact. In the study by Sonesson et al. (2009), the authors discuss cleaner egg production measures. The feed consumption per kilograms of eggs must not exceed 2 kilograms per cage hen, and 2.2 kilograms per free range egg. It is also good practice to regularly analyze the nutrient content of the feed and to only purchase feed from environmental certified producers. Ethically speaking and as discussed previously, it might also be an improvement to phase out non-free-range eggs.

The emissions of ammonia and dinitrogen oxide are the greatest concern in the hen manure management. In order to diminish such emissions the manure could be better tapped. The egg laying farms should be able to present a balance of the farm's nitrogen flow, from feed to eggs, spent laying hens and manure.
19: <header>9. Recommendations </header>

Four sources have been detected as severe emission sources that cause the major environmental impact in the life cycle of the egg phospholipids. These are: hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production, hen manure management and the final egg phospholipids production. Fresenius-Kabi has only direct control over its own final egg phospholipids production. That phase is therefore the only one that the company can directly act upon.

The solvent emissions from the egg phospholipids production is the major environmental problem at the commodity plant in Kungsängen. It is also a greater emission source in the life cycle of egg phospholipids, thus actions that decrease these emissions will also have effects in a life cycle perspective. The carbon dioxide emission from the steam boilers is not as clear emission source as the solvents in a life cycle perspective.

The three other emission sources cannot directly be affected by Fresenius-Kabi. However, the company can demand that certain production conditions are fulfilled or ensured. When analyzing ethanol feedstocks the ethanol yield per square meter is an essential parameter,
which lowers energy, pesticide and fertilizer usage per liter ethanol. Also, the crop properties could be interesting; since some crops are less dependent on fertilizers and pesticides.

Regarding the egg production, requirements should be imposed on the egg yolk powder supplier to only purchase eggs from hen laying farms that work actively to decrease their environmental impact. In the study by Sonesson et al. (2009), the authors discuss cleaner egg production measures. The feed consumption per kilograms of eggs must not exceed 2 kilograms per cage hen, and 2.2 kilograms per free range egg. 20: <header>10. Conclusion </header>

This thesis' aim was to identify and quantify the most severe environmental effects in a life cycle perspective of Fresenius-Kabi's product egg phospholipids. That aim has been reached by using LCA methodology with the ReCiPe Midpoint Hierarchist World impact assessment method. Eleven impact categories have been excluded due to low interest from the employer.

The identified processes that generate the greatest impact in the life cycle of the product are the hen feed and ethanol feedstock production, the hen manure handing and the egg phospholipids production. The most severe environmental impacts are human toxicity and marine eutrophication.

Human toxicity is the impact category where greatest global impact occurs. Pesticide and fertilizers usage in the cultivation of hen feed and ethanol feedstocks generate emissions of phosphorus, manganese and arsenic, which are toxic to humans.

The marine eutrophication impact category finds its emission sources in the feedstocks production of hen feed, especially in the wheat cultivation and in the hen manure management. This impact occurs due to nitrate emissions from the fertilizer and manure handling. The greatest emissions substance source in this impact category is nitrate.

The freshwater eutrophication impact category is dominated by phosphate emissions, which originates from hen manure handling, fertilizer usage in hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production and waste water from the egg phospholipids production, which also contains some phosphorus. The nitrogen production also generates some phosphate emissions.
Other impact categories such as climate change, photochemical oxidant formation, terrestrial acidification and fossil depletion have lower global impact. In
Table 17, the emission quantities are included for the selected impact categories.

Five scenarios are conducted to verify the results and to investigate the environmental impact of other raw materials. Lowering the hen feed intake by 0.14 kilograms per kilograms of egg, gives a two to six percentage reduction in the characterized results. Lowest reduction is yield in acidification and eutrophication impact categories. To produce ethanol from cellulosic feedstocks such as wood or grass show a lower impact in human toxicity, photochemical oxidant formation and freshwater eutrophication. However, grass ethanol shows higher impact in terrestrial acidification. Ethanol produced by wood cellulose has shown the best results, but generates slightly higher emissions of carbon dioxide equivalent and a somewhat higher use of fossil fuels. In the last scenario, the egg yolk powder allocation factor is investigated in a sensitivity analysis. This life cycle phase already has low environmental impact, thus to increase such allocation factor from 11 to 25 percent does not show any significant effect on the final results.

The third and fourth scenarios investigate treatment method of egg yolk residue and hen manure. When avoiding soybean production, the egg yolk residue credits the egg phospholipids more than processing it in a biogas digester, despite the high biogas yield from the egg yolk residue. No obvious best method has been found for hen manure treatment. The used allocation factor to divide egg yolk powder production impacts between the production's products is 11 percent. Such a number is calculated by the supplier. Another allocation factor could be 25 percent, which is the mass of the egg yolk. However, by applying such a factor does not change the final results significantly since the impacts from such life cycle phase is low.

The scenarios show that, by analyzing the environmental impact of the purchased raw materials, Fresenius-Kabi can obtain an egg phospholipids' life cycle production with great environmental impact reduction. Since vast environmental impacts are generated in the hen manure handling as well as in the hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production, the company can impose procurements requirement in feed intake per kilogram of eggs and to demand certifications which ensure low fertilizer and pesticide usage. The egg production can also diminish its impacts by optimizing the manure handling thus less ammonia and nitrate leak. Finally, cleaner production measures that Fresenius-Kabi can take in its own production are analyze its solvent, carbon dioxide, phosphorus and phosphate emissions, which also are high in a life cycle perspective.

24: <header>10. Conclusion </header>

This thesis' aim was to identify and quantify the most severe environmental effects in a life cycle perspective of Fresenius-Kabi's product egg phospholipids. That aim has been reached by using LCA methodology with the ReCiPe Midpoint Hierarchist World impact assessment method. Eleven impact categories have been excluded due to low interest from the employer.

The identified processes that generate the greatest impact in the life cycle of the product are the hen feed and ethanol feedstock production, the hen manure handing and the egg phospholipids production. The most severe environmental impacts are human toxicity and marine eutrophication.

Human toxicity is the impact category where greatest global impact occurs. Pesticide and fertilizers usage in the cultivation of hen feed and ethanol feedstocks generate emissions of phosphorus, manganese and arsenic, which are toxic to humans.

The marine eutrophication impact category finds its emission sources in the feedstocks production of hen feed, especially in the wheat cultivation and in the hen manure management. This impact occurs due to nitrate emissions from the fertilizer and manure handling. The greatest emissions substance source in this impact category is nitrate.

The freshwater eutrophication impact category is dominated by phosphate emissions, which originates from hen manure handling, fertilizer usage in hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production and waste water from the egg phospholipids production, which also contains some phosphorus. The nitrogen production also generates some phosphate emissions.
Other impact categories such as climate change, photochemical oxidant formation, terrestrial acidification and fossil depletion have lower global impact. In
Table 17, the emission quantities are included for the selected impact categories.

Five scenarios are conducted to verify the results and to investigate the environmental impact of other raw materials. Lowering the hen feed intake by 0.14 kilograms per kilograms of egg, gives a two to six percentage reduction in the characterized results. Lowest reduction is yield in acidification and eutrophication impact categories. To produce ethanol from cellulosic feedstocks such as wood or grass show a lower impact in human toxicity, photochemical oxidant formation and freshwater eutrophication. However, grass ethanol shows higher impact in terrestrial acidification. Ethanol produced by wood cellulose has shown the best results, but generates slightly higher emissions of carbon dioxide equivalent and a somewhat higher use of fossil fuels. In the last scenario, the egg yolk powder allocation factor is investigated in a sensitivity analysis. This life cycle phase already has low environmental impact, thus to increase such allocation factor from 11 to 25 percent does not show any significant effect on the final results.

The third and fourth scenarios investigate treatment method of egg yolk residue and hen manure. When avoiding soybean production, the egg yolk residue credits the egg phospholipids more than processing it in a biogas digester, despite the high biogas yield from the egg yolk residue. No obvious best method has been found for hen manure treatment. The used allocation factor to divide egg yolk powder production impacts between the production's products is 11 percent. Such a number is calculated by the supplier. Another allocation factor could be 25 percent, which is the mass of the egg yolk. However, by applying such a factor does not change the final results significantly since the impacts from such life cycle phase is low.

The scenarios show that, by analyzing the environmental impact of the purchased raw materials, Fresenius-Kabi can obtain an egg phospholipids' life cycle production with great environmental impact reduction. Since vast environmental impacts are generated in the hen manure handling as well as in the hen feed and ethanol feedstocks production, the company can impose procurements requirement in feed intake per kilogram of eggs and to demand certifications which ensure low fertilizer and pesticide usage. The egg production can also diminish its impacts by optimizing the manure handling thus less ammonia and nitrate leak. Finally, cleaner production measures that Fresenius-Kabi can take in its own production are analyze its solvent, carbon dioxide, phosphorus and phosphate emissions, which also are high in a life cycle perspective.
